rows.title: Generating Evidence for Future Skills Needs of Migrant Workers in Nepal
rows.description: IOM Nepal will build on the recommendations and gaps identified on its existing studies on skills profiling and reintegration and conduct an in-depth and extensive macro level study to understand the supply and demand part of the skills. The study will map existing training institutions in Nepal, register the type of trainings provided and market diagnostics on the recruitment of trained personnel by private/public sector in Nepal and the role of private recruitment agencies in this process. This will consider pre and post COVID-19 situations and how the training institutes have realigned to meet the demands of COVID affected markets. It will assess employment trends in skilled, semi-skilled and lower-skilled categories of demands in various destinations, map new and emerging job market in various destination and identify key areas and sectors including green job or the one that promotes green economy that will align with Nepal’s human resources or based on its ongoing skills programme. It will also provide the list of skills areas in which further trainings could be considered for high earning of the Nepali migrant workers and government initiatives at the national level to address social protection needs of the returnee migrant workers.

rows.title: Promoting Regular and Orderly Pathways for Gender Responsive, Safe and Secure Migration in Nepal (PROGRESS)
rows.description: Building on the Government’s ongoing initiative to develop a national labour migration policy and national migration policy, IOM Nepal, through the proposed project aims to support the integration of the migration policy at different levels (Outcome 1) and to promote gender-responsive regular pathways for migration with countries of destination in order to address current and future skills needs (Outcome 2). Under the first outcome, the project will promote exchange on the new migration policy (Output 1.1), support dissemination of the policy to provincial governments (Output 1.2) and capacitate media stakeholders to report accurately on the policy and migration-related issues (Output 1.3). Under the second outcome, the project will conduct an assessment on regular pathways in the Nepal-GCC corridor (Output 2.1) and will host a dialogue between the Government of Nepal and GCC country representatives (Output 2.2) and strengthen collaboration with the private sector (Output 2.3), with the aim to contribute to the facilitation of regular migration through improved evidence-based migration dialogue and policy coordination amongst stakeholders in Nepal, and with key countries of destination, in alignment with international standards and national frameworks.

rows.title: Effective Case Management by Strengthening Isolation Centers and Ground Crossing Points (GCPs) Management for Rapid Response and Preparedness against COVID-19
rows.description: During the second wave of COVID-19, 13 government-designated ground crossing points (GCPs) were allocated for the entry and exit along the India-Nepal border with health desks for screening and testing. Assessments have shown that GCP management is insufficient and they lacked dedicated isolation centers, resulting in severe positive cases being sent to nearby hospitals and mild/moderate cases sent for home quarantine, risking further spread of the virus. Based on the assessment results, IOM proposes to strengthen the capacities of six GCPs and isolation centers through supporting the Governments of Province 1, 2, Lumbini and Sudurpaschim province, supporting the government in their ongoing interventions at the GCPs and isolation centers, including provision of materials, capacity building and human resources, sustainable WASH facilities and ensuring protection of vulnerable groups. Displacement Tracking Matrix (DTM) will be implemented for flow monitoring including at informal GCPs.

rows.title: NRREP: National Rural and Renewable Energy Programme
rows.description:
The National Rural and Renewable Energy Programme (NRREP) will support renewable energy in rural areas through renewable energy technologies such as, solar, micro hydro, biogas and improved cooking stoves. The objective is to reach 1.5 mio households with special focus gender equality and social inclusion.

NRREP has a clear emphasis on effectively reaching out to the more remote and poorest part of the country, it will apply demand led approaches actively involving beneficiaries in decision making, and support use of energy for productive purposes leading to income and employment increase in rural areas. 

Structure: the programme has two national partners and consists of three components. The Alternative Energy Promotion Centre (AEPC) is the executing agency for two of the components: Technical Suppoort Component and the Business Developmnt for Renewable Energy and Productive Energy Use Component. Central Renewable Energy Fund (CREF) is the other national partner and the establishment of the Fund is an independent component. NRREP is a single programme modality, which means that no other programmes within renewable energy in Nepal are to be funded outside NRREP to avoid duplication and lack of coordination.

The immediate objective of the Central Renewable Energy Fund Component is to institute the CREF as the core financial institution responsible for the effective delivery of subsidies and credit support to the renewable energy sector.

The immediate objective of the Technical Support Component is to accelerate renewable energy service delivery with better quality, comprising various technologies, to remote rural households, enterprises and communities, to benefit men and women from all social groups, leading to more equitable economic growth.

The immediate objective of the Business Development for Renewable Energy and Productive Energy Use Component is to contribute to an increase in income and employment generation potential for MSMEs in rural area.

rows.title: Nepal: Promoting A Human Security and Gender Equality-Based Approach to Migration and Adaptation to Climate Change
rows.description: Nepal witnesses high human mobility in the form of both voluntary and forced migration. The country has extremely high levels of climate risk. Climate change and human mobility is a complex and multifaceted phenomenon that cuts across different policy areas, including but not limited to migration, development, climate change and environment, humanitarian assistance and security. The Government of Nepal is keen to address the issue in an integrated and coherent manner to provide solutions for people to move, people on the move and people to stay. The objective of the project is to support the Government of Nepal’s efforts to respond to challenges along the MECC nexus in a human security-centred and gender responsive manner. The project aims to further strengthen the knowledge base to inform government stakeholders and influence policies related to climate change, disasters, gender, and migration. Along with this, the project will support local governments to draft plans, adopting a human security approach, for gender responsive safe, orderly and regular migration and adaptation to climate change. By engaging with different spheres of government and engaging leading civil society organisations (CSOs) and communities, the project will promote participatory, joint approaches to analyse and address drivers that compel people to move and support the Government in taking climate actions and enhancing adaptive capacities of the population by making migration a choice.

rows.title: Enhancing Migration Governance in Nepal: A Collaborative Approach for Strengthening Coordination Mechanism and Knowledge Management (ENGIN)
rows.description: Building upon the partnership between Deutsche Gesellschaft für Internationale Zusammenarbeit (GIZ) GmbH and IOM globally, this project aims to strengthen Nepal's migration governance through enhanced coordination mechanisms, and the promotion of safe, orderly and regular migration practices. The project envisages collaborative efforts among the government, civil society actors, private sector and academia, fostering a deepened understanding of migration impacts and the integration of safe migration practices across sectors. The project will support the government with the development of a comprehensive GCM National Implementation Strateguc Framework. This will involve the establishment of a multistakeholder coordination mechanism and knowledge sharing and management through Migration School. Policy briefs and dialogues on migration and development will be important outputs. The project prioritizes gender mainstreaming, ensuring a gender-responsive approach in capacity-building, policy dialogues, and ethical recruitment practices. It also emphasizes inclusivity by considering the youth and addressing their challenges, promoting environmental sustainability through climate change adaptation, and ensuring benefits extend to those left behind. The emphasis on regular pathways promotes legal and safe migration, contributing to a transparent and secure framework. These cross-cutting considerations enhance the overall effectiveness, inclusivity, and sustainability of migration governance initiatives in Nepal.

rows.title: Nepal: School on Migration Studies (SMS)
rows.description: In recent years, migrants have made a significant contribution to the socio-economic landscape of Nepal. However, as migration and sustainable development is an issue which has not yet been addressed in the curricula of Nepali universities, there exists a gap in academic knowledge and a small number of policymakers with expertise on the matter. As a result, stakeholders such as academicians, policymakers and practitioners have limited exposure on an overarching comprehensive and harmonized approach to migration for development. Migration governance thus remains to be mainstreamed in the policies of all three tiers (federal, provincial and local level) of government. In light of the above, the objective of this project is to support the Government of Nepal in addressing migration issues in a comprehensive manner for the benefit of sustainable development through the acquisition of migration knowledge.The project will seek to do so by engaging the government, civil society actors and members of academia to work collaboratively in the field of migration and improve their understanding on the complexity of migration impacts in Nepal. To that end, the project will: establish and hold two editions of the School on Migration Studies (SMS), which will provide an interactive course for the benefit of the aforementioned stakeholders to strengthen their knowledge on migration and sustainable development, addressing various thematic areas on migration; provide grants for students and young researchers to produce novel and local migration research; and produce recommendations to identify and address knowledge gaps, and assess training needs in the field of migration research.

rows.title: Mapping of Migrant Vulnerabilities and Support Services in Nepal
rows.description: The objective of this project is to contribute to the efforts of the Government of Nepal to manage cross-border mobility and apply evidence-based policy to better protect vulnerable migrants, including victims of trafficking. To achieve the objective, the project will carry out a mapping of migration flows at key border points with India and publish an analysis of the data with a specific focus on migrant vulnerabilities. The project will also review current policies to address cross-border migration and the measures and actions currently implemented on national, provincial and local levels in regard to irregular migration, assess their compatibility with international standards and best practices, and map out cross border migration trends. Additionally, the project will roll out IOM's Reintegration Handbook, train relevant stakeholders including migrant networks/groups and NGOs at local levels to provide sustainable reintegration services to vulnerable migrants.

rows.title: Joint Recovery Action Plan (JRAP) - Western Nepal Earthquake
rows.description: According to the National Emergency Operations Centre, 62,000 homes were affected (35,455 partially damaged and 26,557 completely damaged) by the earthquake which struck Jajarkot district in Karnali province in November 2023. To address the temporary shelter needs of affected families by three earthquakes of Jajarkot, Bajura and Doti, the Government of Nepal endorsed the Temporary Housing Construction Procedure for Earthquake Affected Households and pledged 1.41 billion Nepalese rupees to support reconstruction and recovery efforts. Through this project, the International Organization for Migration is supporting the Government of Nepal in creating an enabling environment for the long-term recovery and reconstruction of earthquake-affected communities. The project is contributing towards the empowerment of affected populations and those in vulnerable situations to become agents of their own recovery through rebuilding their homes as well as reducing future disaster risks.

rows.title: UNNATI-Inclusive Growth Programme in Nepal
rows.description:
The key priority in the programme is to strengthen inclusive market based growth to reduce poverty and raise living standards. The programme will target commercialisation of agricultural products and upgrade rural infrastructure in Eastern Nepal. Also addressing the framework for inclusive growth at political levels will be key to facilitating sustainable, inclusive growth. 400 million DKK have been allocated for a five-year period commencing January 2014. 

The programme design includes three components, each with two sub-components: The Value Chain Component (with sub-components Access to Finance and Commercialisation of Value Chains; The Infrastructure Component (with sub-components Rural roads and Bridges and Market Infrastructure); and The Enabling Environment Component (with sub-components Private-Public Dialogue and Advocacy for Rights and CSR).

To support the implementation of sub-component 1.1 Commercialisation of Selected Value Chains, and the entire Infrastructure Component, the programme will make use of a management contractor. Other implementing partners include Local government, Nepal Rastra Bank together with UNCDF, Nepal Business Forum, and an advocacy fund manager.

rows.title: EnDev Energy Enterprise Coach
rows.description: The EnDev Energy Enterprise Coach (EEC) provides business development services (BDS) to renewable energy enterprises (encompassing at least 25% women owned), with the aim to support their professionalisation and thereby aspiring to scale operations and become investment ready in Africa and Asia. This is achieved by being a one-stop-shop facility for demand-driven interventions tailored to an enterprise's stage of growth, technology offering, context and location.

rows.title: EnDev Global Enabling Environment Facility (GEEF): Strengthening the enabling environment for Energy Access SMEs
rows.description: Via a pool of experts, EnDev country teams can request advisory services related to enabling environment activities. Via national trajectories, technical assistance is offered on different enabling environment related topics, like strategy and policy development, regulatory and tax measures, quality standards (enforcement).

rows.title: Review of EnDev Data Quality
rows.description: Energising Development (EnDev) is an international flagship programme for providing energy access. The objective of the EnDev approach is to facilitate market development by means of interventions in demand, supply and enabling environment in its partner countries. Since its inception in 2005, EnDev results are monitored and reported. EnDev’s monitoring approach is to quantify results, continuously improve methodologies aligned to international debates and developments, increase efficiency and transparency whilst ensuring robustness and accuracy.The objectives of this assignment are to review and further develop EnDev’s current monitoring system in theory and practice with a focus on data quality, by accessing data collection processes and consolidating verification practices into a standardised process. This two-part assignment focusses on 1.	The review of monitoring processes including data collection, verification of results, and data aggregation and quality control; and2.	The operationalisation of two selected result types:a. CO2 emission savings andb. Job creation.

rows.title: EnDev Strategic Stocktake
rows.description: This assignment is aimed at guiding the EnDev HQ Management Team, supervised by the EnDev Consultative Group (BMZ, DGIS, Norad, SDC), through a strategic stocktake process to identify the most impactful areas where and how energy access activities would contribute significantly towards the achievement of SDG 7.The assignment's objective is to create an overview of current initiatives and achievements on energy access in sub-Saharan Africa, relevant policy priorities of the EnDev Consultative Group, other SDG 7 donors and partner countries and interventions and modalities needed to achieve SDG 7 in the run-up to 2030.

rows.title: EnDev Adaptation Impact Assignment
rows.description: The objective of this assignment is to assess and describe adaptation impact induced by EnDev interventions. The assignment would focus on four use-cases: 1) powering ventilation; 2) water pumping / irrigation; 3) powering communication; and 4) clean cooking. The assignment will include case studies in two EnDev countries that are highly vulnerable to climate change and where chances are high that measurable impact may be achieved through EnDev activities. In these countries and for these use-cases, the assignment aims at first describing the links between the activities and impacts and providing more detailed insights about the magnitude of EnDev’s contribution to adaptation to climate change of EnDev target groups. Furthermore, the assignment aims to investigate which indicators would be relevant to measure adaptation impact for these use-cases and related additional monitoring requirements. Thirdly, the assignment includes the identification of relevant partners in the climate adaptation-energy interconnection, at national and international level. Finally, the study aims to identify potential future supplementary measures of EnDev activities to augment adaptation impacts.

rows.title: Further development, update, maintenance and usage of the Energy Access Market Development (EAMD) Scorecard
rows.description: The project makes sures that the Energy Access Market Development (EAMD) Scorecards are methodologically sound, regularly updated, and actively uses the methodology in the Energising Development (EnDev), Strengthening the Entrepreneurial Ecosystem for Clean Cooking (SEE Clean Cooking) and SDG7 Results programme.

rows.title: Access to Finance Advisory
rows.description: The objective of this assignment was to obtain external expertise to feed into the design of a range of Access to Finance activities and programming.Specifically, it sought to gather insights, practical recommendations, and specialised knowledge from experienced professionals to strengthen strategic planning, enhance implementation approaches, and ensure that proposed interventions were relevant, effective, and sustainable.Ultimately, this assignment helped shape the Access to Finance initiatives that were later developed and implemented under EnDev.

rows.title: EnDev Evaluation 2025-2026 – Insights for Impact: Harvesting, evaluating and learning
rows.description: The objective of this evaluation is, on the one hand, to generate learnings which contribute to the knowledge gained from previous evaluations. The aim is to complete the evaluation of the EnDev portfolio when it comes to effectiveness, relevance, coherence, sustainability and impact and to shed special light on the newest strategic priorities, and by doing so to close the gap between what was already concluded by previous studies and what was still not evaluated in depth. On the other hand, this evaluation will be forward-looking, assessing the EnDev instruments against the needs of the sector and build on these learnings to benefit the sector as a whole and for a potential future programme.

rows.title: Dutch Spark - Netherlands 'Community of practice' promotion of clean cooking for everybody
rows.description: The Dutch Spark is a unique collaboration between Netherlands-based public, business, finance, carbon, and development partners to accelerate the transition to clean cooking.Founded in 2015 as a Community of Practice, Dutch Spark has since made a constructive contribution to highlighting Dutch knowledge and expertise regarding clean and efficient cooking, and has developed into an important network for knowledge exchange.

rows.title: EnDev Real Time Evaluation
rows.description: EnDev Programme Management, under the mandate of the Consultative Group, contracted the consortium PricewaterhouseCoopers Advisory N.V. (PwC), Vrije Universiteit Amsterdam (VUA) and SEO Economics (SEO) to perform a real-time evaluation of the EnDev programme between 2020 and 2023.The three evaluation objectives were to1. assess EnDev against the OECD-DAC criteria and additionality,2. assess the management's fit for purpose, and3. use the results for strategic learning, in order to allow EnDev to refine and reinforce its strategy and implementation.To meet the evaluation's objectives, a performance evaluation and a process evaluation were conducted. The evaluation had a mixed-method nature, and used monitoring data analysis, in-depth case studies, a stakeholder survey, interviews and a systematic literature review.

rows.title: Further development, update, maintenance and application of the Energy Access Market Development (EAMD) Scorecard
rows.description: The project makes sures that the Energy Access Market Development (EAMD) Scorecards are methodologically sound, regularly updated, and actively uses the methodology in the Energising Development (EnDev), Strengthening the Entrepreneurial Ecosystem for Clean Cooking (SEE Clean Cooking) and SDG7 Results programmes.

rows.title: EnDev Innovation and Learning Agenda – Innovative Finance
rows.description: To spur and structure learning and knowledge exchange within EnDev and the SDG 7 community, the EnDev Learning & Innovation Agenda (ELIA) was created in 2020, to facilitate learning and knowledge sharing via regular exchange meetings, as well as to develop knowledge products based on shared experience and research.ELIA encompasses the set-up of a Community of Practice (COP) on specific thematic topics, including Innovative Finance for Energy Access, through which EnDev aims to enhance literacy around the financial tools for energy access, identify important innovations that contribute to closing the financing gap for SDG 7, and understand where more innovation is needed.

rows.title: Investigation into the impact on biodiversity of Clean Cooking activities
rows.description: Under this contract, expertise was mobilised to investigatge the degree to which clean cooking activities contribute towards preservation of biodiversity.

rows.title: Innovation Window: Digital innovations for eCooking
rows.description: The project, a collaboration between Practical Action, the National Association of Community Electricity Users' Nepal (NACEUN), and the Ajummery Bikas Foundation (ABF), with funding support through the EnDev Innovation Window of the Netherlands Enterprise Agency (RVO) was implemented in two districts of the Gandaki Province, Parbhat and Syangja. The digital platforms developed are aimed at accelerating the eCooking market.The project focuses on three key areas: 1.	The development of web/mobile-based digital applications for eCooking market actors and users;2.	Capacity building of eCooking users and supply chain actors in utilising digital applications for informed decision-making; and 3.	Establishing linkages between demand and supply chain actors to promote eCooking market development. The project is perceived to be a commendable attempt to introduce digital platforms in the clean cooking sector. Although the number of cookstove sales through digital platforms remained at just about two-thirds of the target, the overall information reach exceeded the numbers.The project successfully promoted digital innovations on both the demand and supply sides. It had a positive impact on users' perception of eCooking technology, empowering rural women in decision-making, and promoting a clean kitchen environment for end users.The project's implementation demonstrated efficiency, offering cost-effectiveness and time-saving benefits through the digital platform. Sustainability can be achieved through the platform's adaptability and stakeholder collaboration.

rows.title: Gender and Energy Innovation Facility
rows.description: The Gender and Energy Innovation Facility (GEIF) aimed to spur innovation for gender transformation in the energy sector. By generating new insights on addressing persistent challenges to provide access to energy in a gender inclusive way, possible (scalable) solutions were identified. GEIF was implemented by Hivos, the Energising Development programme (EnDev) and the Modern Energy Cooking Services (MECS), and included funding from SIDA.Local and regional organisations in Kenya, Nepal and Tanzania were invited to apply, join 5-days innovation bootcamps to develop their idea, and finally submit their developed idea in a concept note for selection. Ten projects were implemented, four from Kenya, three from Nepal, and three from Tanzania. Overall conclusions that can be drawn at the end of the GEIF is that the pilot project implementers have enhanced their knowledge and understanding of gender concepts in their sector, and succeeded in 'shifting the needle' towards gender equality in the energy sector.The areas that demonstrated the highest potential for long-term impact are:•	The development of new products or services associated with clean energy.•	Access to clean(er) energy.•	The empowerment of women via upskilling them in traditionally male-dominated roles and sectors, for instance by training them in repairing and maintaining induction stoves or installing solar technology.

rows.title: Dutch Spark – Netherlands 'Community of practice' promotion of clean cooking for everybody
rows.description: Dutch Spark is a unique collaboration between Netherlands-based public, business, finance, carbon, and development partners. Its primary goal is to accelerate the transition to clean cooking.The organisation functions as a community of practice, acquiring and sharing knowledge, creating support, and demonstrating successful concepts in practice.The mission of Dutch Spark is to contribute to the accessibility of clean and efficient cooking solutions, with a preference for non-fossil fuels, for all. The vision of the organisation is that by 2030, everyone will have access to affordable clean cooking solutions, with the cleanest and most efficient technologies possible.Dutch Spark is also involved in various projects, such as 'Partners in Pellets' in Rwanda, which aims to develop the market for pellets in combination with using a clean gasifier.Dutch Spark operates in various regions and countries, including East Asia and the Pacific, South Asia, Latin America, North Africa and Sub-Saharan Africa.RemarkDue to the transparency with regard to the IATI projects, a non-ODA country has been included in this project.More information: Dutch Spark for clean cooking solutionsUrl = https://dutch-spark.org/

rows.reporting: Foreign Affairs, Trade and Development Canada (DFATD)
rows.title: Global Human Rights Education Program
rows.description: The project aims to strengthen local and regional capacity for human rights education in developing countries and countries in transition. Building on the achievements of Equitas' annual training of approximately 120 human rights educators from around the world, the project also implements new country-based, focussed initiatives on local priorities that consolidate and expand on earlier results. Equitas employs a methodology developed and refined over the years, which is participatory and learner-centered.   Equitas works with key organizations representing local and national governments, human rights institutions and civil society. It has made progress in its efforts to build new Canadian, international and multilateral partnerships and has established partnerships with a number of international institutions and multilateral agencies such as the United Nations Office of the High Commissioner for Human Rights, United Nations Development Program and the Organization for Security and Cooperation in Europe.

rows.reporting: Foreign Affairs, Trade and Development Canada (DFATD)
rows.title: Sahakarya - Building Self-Reliant Communities - I
rows.description: Sahakarya aims to improve socio-economic conditions of selected communities by promoting equitable and representative community-based organizations (CBOs) capable of managing local resources and interacting with locally elected bodies and government agencies.   The goal of the project is to contribute to the reduction of poverty in the hilly regions of Nepal by strengthening community-based organizations (CBOs) through economic and social development. The project is being implemented in the Mid and Far Western Districts: Jumla, Dadeldhura, Baitadi, Surkhet, and Dailekh. The Canadian Executing Agency is Canadian Centre for Studies and International Cooperation (CECI) and the Social Welfare Council (SWC) is the Nepalese government counterpart.

rows.reporting: Foreign Affairs, Trade and Development Canada (DFATD)
rows.title: Sahakarya - Building Self-Reliant Communities - II
rows.description: Sahakarya aims to improve socio-economic condition of selected communities by promoting equitable and representative community-based organizations (CBOs) capable of managing local resources and interacting with locally elected bodies and government agencies.   The goal of the project is to contribute to the reduction of poverty in the hilly regions of Nepal by strengthening community-based organizations (CBOs) through economic and social development. The project is being implemented in the Mid and Far Western Districts: Jumla, Dadeldhura, Baitadi, Surkhet, and Dailekh, focusing on market-oriented agricultural production. The Canadian Executing Agency is the Centre for Studies and International Cooperation (CECI) and the Social Welfare Council (SWC) is the Nepalese government counterpart.

rows.title: Child Labour-Free Carpets – Deep Due Diligence for Dutch Companies
rows.description: This project aims to expand and broaden a comprehensive solution to child labour in carpet manufacturing supply chains in India and Nepal, which will, in turn, strengthen corporate due diligence of Dutch retailers and wholesalers in line with the Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development (OECD)’s guidelines and recent human rights legislation. According to GoodWeave’s nearly three decades of experience in the carpet sector in South Asia, more than 90% of all child labour cases are identified in subcontracted production, where visibility is scarce and monitoring efforts do not reach informal worksites, enabling child labour to remain undetected.By engaging Dutch companies that source from at-risk hotspots, GoodWeave will establish supply chain transparency, down to the most remote home-based worksites, identify cases of child labour and at-risk children in these hotspots, and build capacity of brands, producers and local partners to address cases through remediation and prevention programmes.  Activities include supply chain mapping, inspection and monitoring of both child labour and linked risks, such as wage levels; remediation of identified child labour cases; implementation of child labour prevention programming; and building of replicable examples for corporate conduct to address child labour. Capacity building will be provided to participating brands and in-country producers to ensure strengthened child labour policies and procedures, and verification of their effectiveness. The project also includes company engagement and public communications that promote partner companies as models and tell the stories of former child labourers. This will connect consumers and other market players with the impact that their choices have on maker communities, thus supporting project sustainability. The long-term vision is to strengthen participating companies’ and producers' due diligence in carpet production in India and Nepal to insulate their supply chains from child labour.

rows.reporting: Foreign Affairs, Trade and Development Canada (DFATD)
rows.title: Peace-Building from Below
rows.description: The project facilitates the mobilization, development, strengthening, and networking of community-based organizations (CBOs) and people's organizations in two districts in eastern Nepal that lack basic infrastructure, government resources, and social sector support systems. The project focuses on promoting equitable socio-economic development by empowering local people, particularly marginalized and disadvantaged groups. It aims to improve health and sanitation in rural communities by increasing livelihood and employment opportunities for women, Dalits, and other disadvantaged groups. The project includes advocating and networking for social justice, good governance, and pro-poor development. It supports democracy programming by addressing the needs of internally displaced persons (IDPs) and ex-combatants, especially women, who are returning home after years of conflict. The project is implemented by Rural Reconstruction Nepal, in partnership with local CBOs, non-governmental organizations, and relevant government agencies.

rows.reporting: Foreign Affairs, Trade and Development Canada (DFATD)
rows.title: Canada Fund for Local Initiatives (CFLI) - Nepal - 2011-2012
rows.description: The Canada Fund for Local Initiatives (CFLI) is designed to support small projects proposed and implemented by local organizations in Nepal. In providing funding for small projects that offer direct social, economic, or technical assistance to local populations, the CFLI contributes to the overall goal of reducing poverty.   The objective of this CFLI is to support Nepal's peace process by promoting community reconciliation and enhanced community economic opportunities. It also supports the protection and promotion of human rights. The main organizations eligible for funding are community-based organizations and national level non-governmental organizations.

rows.title: PRG-Nepal Peace, Rights and Governance Programme
rows.description:
The Peace Rights and Governance programme (PRGP) is designed to support initiatives by state and non-state actors, the sole aim of which is to foster social cohesion and thereby help Nepal to ensure political stability and peace in the medium term. The promotion of conflict transformation is a central objective of all programme components, sub-components and projects at all levels and across all aspects of the PRGP. 

The program has a total budget of DKK 200 mio. for a period of 5 years from 2014-2018. 

The program comprises of 3 components: I) Peace building and democratic development II) Local Governance and III) Access to Justice and human rights. 

Component I will be implemented by the Nepal Peace Trust Fund (NPTF), Election Commission and the multi donor Governance Facility (GF). Component II will be implemented by Ministry of Federal Affairs and local development. Component III will be implemented by National Human Rights Commission, the Governance Facility and other partners. 

Different interventions designed under the PRGP will contribute to peace-building, improve the effectiveness of the state and increase partnerships with civil society.

rows.title: Oxfam Novib Cultivating Change 2024 - 2027 - Oxfam Novib Cultivating Change 2024 - 2027 GLOBEC
rows.description:
Den övergripande målsättningen för Cultivating Change är att bidra till att kvinnor på landsbygden och deras samhällen har ökad motståndskraft, mat/livsmedel, näring och inkomstsäkerhet och har tillsammans med sina rörelser makten att omvandla/förbättra de befintliga systemen som karakteriseras av ojämlikhet och kränkningar av rättigheter som orsakar hunger, negativa klimateffekter och ekologiskt sammanbrott. Målsättningen bidrar till tre utmaningar: hållbar livsmedelsproduktion inom de planetära gränserna (SDG 1, 2, 12, 13, 15), resiliensutmaningen (SDG 2, 13, 15) och jämlikhetsutmaningen i livsmedelsvärdekedjan (SDG 5, 8).

Slutresultaten förväntas vara att:

1. Kvinnor på landsbygden och deras samhällen har ökat mat-, närings- och inkomstsäkerheten och bygger tillsammans med sina rörelser rättvisa, hållbara och motståndskraftiga livsmedelssystem för alla; 

2. Kvinnor på landsbygden och deras samhällen har säkrat mark- och resursrättigheter och leder tillsammans med sina rörelser aktivt inkluderande styrning och hållbar förvaltning av sina naturresurser; samt

3. Kvinnor på landsbygden och deras samhällen har ökat motståndskraften mot klimatförändringar och leder tillsammans med sina rörelser klimatåtgärder.  

Insatsen har ett tydligt fokus på biologisk mångfald inom jordbruket, klimat anpassning kopplat till utvecklingen av lokala fröer/utsäde, påverkansarbete och ansvarsutkrävande av privata och offentliga aktörer kring hållbara och rättvisa livsmedelssystem, samt kvinnors ekonomiska egenmakt och tryggade inkomster. OxfamN har också integrerat nationell, regional och global nivå i upplägget av den nya insatsen, till skillnad från i SeedsGROW där det fanns en projektdel som hanterade nationellt genomförande och en annan på global kampanjnivå.

rows.description: A feasibility study to examine viability of a high quality maternal and newborn health package including technologies and services.The activities will be concentrated around the Dhulikhel Hospital, a not-for-profit, non-government tertiary level hospital, that runs 18 primary level Outreach Clinics and also provides (para)medical training services.The catchment population is the approximately 179 thousand women of the reproductive age and newborns living in the districts of Kavrepalanchok and Sindhupalchok, of which over one fifth (21.69%) is living in poverty.

rows.reporting: Foreign Affairs, Trade and Development Canada (DFATD)
rows.title: Local Governance and Community Development Program - core component
rows.description: The project goal is to contribute to poverty reduction and economic growth through inclusive, responsible and accountable local governance and participatory community-led development. The program purpose is to improve access to locally and inclusively prioritised public goods and services. This is being achieved by investing in rural development, mobilizing local resources and involving communities in local development and planning.   Activities being supported include building roads, irrigation systems and electrification systems which aim to reach all 3,915 villages in Nepal. The Local Governance and Community Development Program (LGCDP) is a national multi-donor program, being implemented by the Government of Nepal's Ministry of Local Development.

rows.reporting: Foreign Affairs, Trade and Development Canada (DFATD)
rows.title: Local Governance and Community Development Program - Monitoring and Advisory Support
rows.description: This project represents funds set aside for monitoring and evaluation of the Local Governance and Community Development Program, as well as for advisory support. For administrative reasons, sometimes the funds identified for this purpose are included in the main project budget and sometimes, as in this case, they are assigned a separate project number.

rows.reporting: Foreign Affairs, Trade and Development Canada (DFATD)
rows.title: Engaging with the Constituent Assembly on Constitutional Development
rows.description: This project builds on and consolidates results achieved during the Developing Democracy in Nepal sub-project. It promotes strategic engagement between civil society organizations and Nepal's Constituent Assembly, responsible for developing the new constitution. The goal of the project is to ensure that the new constitution provides the structure for a strong and vibrant sustainable democracy that protects human rights, promotes economic development and consolidates the peace process in Nepal. The project aims to ensure that the constitution-making process is inclusive, effective and democratic, and that the constitution includes specific provisions relating to fundamental equality rights. The Canadian Bar Association implements this project in partnership with the Nepal Bar Association (NBA), helping to strengthen the NBA's capacity to support the constitution-making process and the implementation of the new constitution. Project activities include: providing advice related to constitutional implementation to the NBA and other Nepali civil society organizations; mobilizing Canadian and international volunteer legal experts; and producing research papers to inform and support recommendations for constitutional provisions relating to fundamental rights.

rows.title: International Centre for Integrated Mountain Dev. (ICIMOD), 2017-2022 - International Centre for Integrated Mountain Dev. (ICIMOD), 2017-2021
rows.description:
ICIMOD has applied for support from Sida amounting to 150 000 000 SEK for implementation of ICIMOD's operations in the Hindu Kush Himalayan region, according to the plan "Medium Term Action Plan, 2018-2022". ICIMOD is an intergovernmental organization which through regional cooperation generates and shares knowledge to promote sustainble development in the region. The organization's member countries are Afghanistan, Bangladesh, Bhutan, China, India, Myanmar, Nepal and Pakistan. The ICIMOD's programs contribute to reduced poverty, reduced physical and social vulnerability and improvement of ecosystem services for men, women and children in the region. The Swedish contribution is given as overall program support in order to harmonize the support as far as possible for the effective implementation of the five-year plan. Program support includes support for regional programs, - innovation and integration as well as institutional functions.
 
Planned strategic results are:
- Dissemination of innovations and practices developed by ICIMOD and partners for adaptation to changes that result in positive results for men, women and children. E.g.: flood early warning systems and climate change adapted value chains.
- Significant progress in the generation and use of relevant data, knowledge and analysis. E.g.: Measure, monitor and manage air pollution levels.
- Significant progress in approaches and knowledge supporting gender equality and inclusive development. E.g.: Strengthen women's capacity and leadership roles in agriculture and local decision making bodies.
- Significant progress in human and institutional capacity. E.g.: Strengthen the member countries' environmental management authorities.
- Significantly affected policies through ICIMOD and partner organizations work. E.g.: contribute to improved environmental legislation in the region.
- Strengthened regional cooperation related to sustainable development in mountain areas. E.g.: promote technical exchange between countries and joint management of transboundary natural resources.
- Global awareness of the importance of mountain areas for improved and resilient living conditions and ecosystems. E.g.: influence at the global climate negotiations.

The Swedish contribution will fund the completion of the current phase in 2017 and a broad program support for ICIMOD's five-year plan 2018-2022. Funding of the new five-year plan is planned with support from 8 donors where Sweden is included. In addition, smaller amounts will be provided from the eight member states. ICIMOD retains approximately 70% of the budget for regional programs and transmits about 30% to partner organizations. Sweden is a longstanding cooperation partner of ICIMOD, and Sweden has during the period 2012-2016 financed two of ICIMOD's initiatives, the climate change adaptation initiative HICAP and the Atmosphere initiative.

ICIMOD performs annual audits of third party organizations that receive funds exceeding SEK 505,000.

The environmental economics network SANDEE, which has for 15 years been supported with good results under Sweden's Strategy for Research Cooperation, will from 2017 be integrated into ICIMOD and take part of Sweden's new programme support to ICIMOD. SANDEE's results will continue to contribute to the Research Strategy.

Sweden will also separately finance a Bilateral Associate Expert (BAE) position at ICIMOD, in the field of water and climate adaptation.

Sweden is a longstanding cooperation partner of ICIMOD, and Sweden has during the period 2012-2016 financed two of ICIMOD's initiatives, the climate change adaptation initiative HICAP and the Atmosphere initiative.

ICIMOD performs annual audits of third party organizations that receive funds exceeding SEK 505,000.

The environmental economics network SANDEE, which has for 15 years been supported with good results under Sweden's Strategy for Research Cooperation, will from 2017 be integrated into ICIMOD and take part of Sweden's new programme support to ICIMOD. SANDEE's results will continue to contribute to the Research Strategy.

Sweden will also separately finance a Bilateral Associate Expert (BAE) position at ICIMOD, in the field of water and climate adaptation.

rows.title: Swedish Mission Council frame 2017-2021 - SMR Swedish Mission Council frame 2017-2021
rows.description:
SMR has submitted an additional application for Sida grants for 2018-2019 in total
122,000,000 SEK.

ADDITIONAL APPLICATION
Prior to this agreement period, the Pentecostal Mission's development cooperation (PMU) has not been given renewed status as a framework organization at Sida and thus no longer receives direct support from the CSO grant. Instead, PMU, which is a member organization of SMR, has chosen to apply for grants for projects in support of civil society in developing countries from SMR, which pass on grants from the CSO grant. Prior to the current sub-agreement for the period 2017-2021, SMR has already received an offer from Sida to supplement it with an additional application for the period 2018-2019, which corresponds to SMR's assessment and recommendations for support to PMU. Subsequently, PMU has submitted to SMR with an application for a grant from SMR for the period 2018-2019.

BUDGET
SMR is applying for a total of SEK 122,000,000 for the two-year period 2018-2019, in addition to the already agreed amount of SEK 790,000,000. This is the same amount that the PMU applied for to SMR. SMR's total budget for the supplementary application 2018-2019 is SEK 134,551,400 (including SEK 12,551,400 in own contribution). The distribution between SMR, their Swedish member organizations and the local partners changes somewhat for the period to which the supplementary application relates. Based on the total budget for the supplementary application 2018-2019, the proportion staying with the Swedish member organizations increases from 13% to 19% and the proportion staying with the local partners decreases correspondingly from 81% to 75%. About 6% still stay with SMR.

BASIS FOR PAGE ASSESSMENT
The preparation has largely been based on SMR's input assessment of PMU's program application, but also SMR's organizational assessment of PMU and Sida's follow-up of SMR and PMU in Kenya, May 2017. Sida has also had access to PMU's program application to SMR, and the PMU's response to SMR's supplementary questions regarding the application.

THE PREPARATION
Since the intervention is prepared as a supplementary intervention decision, the preparation has, as far as possible, been based on the extensive preparation made of the application for the ongoing agreement period 2017-2021. As the assessment made of the application 2017-2021 is still relevant, this has been retained and supplementary comments regarding 2018-2019 have been added where the additional application causes an updated supplementary assessment. The revisions made by the committee therefore only affect the period 2018-2019.

rows.title: Business Event Nepal
rows.description: As a follow-up of the performed market study earlier in 2018, the findings of the market study will be presented to inform Dutch companies and organisations about the trade and investment opportunities and challenges for trade and investment in Nepal.

rows.title: LO/TCO SOUTH 2005-2006
rows.description: The activity does not have a description. You can contact Sida for more information. Call +46 8 698 50 00 or send an e-mail to sida@sida.se.

rows.title: Strengthening the E-commerce debate at the WTO
rows.description: The short-term goal is to organise a side event during the Global Review on Aid for Trade on two relevant topics within e-commerce. These topics are consumer protection and financial inclusion.This project creates a safe and interactive space for self-critical debate and strengthens proactive engagement by developing countries in ongoing e-commerce debates in Geneva.This project links higher-level debates on rules and regulations to practical in-country examples by stakeholders from developing and developed countries, strengthening trust between parties and improving mutual understanding for arguments in a thus far very polarised debate.

rows.title: Strategic Support to Multilateral Trade Policy
rows.description: The project's primary objective is to enhance the prominence of the Netherlands in the deliberations held within the WTO, and to increase awareness among Dutch businesses, especially small- and medium-sized enterprises, about the significance of the WTO.Additionally, this initiative will provide valuable strategic insights into the ongoing multilateral discussions at the WTO, encompassing topics such as e-commerce, Joint Statement Initiatives, and WTO reform.This project will enable a comprehensive understanding of the legal and political implications of the forthcoming cases to be addressed by the WTO Dispute Settlement Body in the upcoming months.

rows.title: ForumCiv frame 2018-2022
rows.description:
Det övergripande målet med Forum Syds insats är att stärkta marginaliserade människor som lever i fattigdom har demokratiskt inflytande och tillgång till rättigheter. Forum Syd vill därmed bidra till förändringar mot rättvisa-, jämställda- och miljömässigt hållbara samhällen. Målet bygger på att det finns behov av att världens samhällen utvecklas mot att bli mer rättvisa, jämställda och miljömässigt hållbara.

För att uppnå det övergripande målet kommer Forum Syd att främst stödja förändringar på tre aktörsnivåer: 1) lokal samarbetspartner, 2) rättighetsinnehavare, och 3) ansvarsbärare. Inom dessa ramar kommer Forum Syd att vidareförmedla medel inom en rad olika tematiska områden och verksamheter, i ett stort antal länder och regioner. Kontexten och de specifika utvecklingsproblemen för respektive insats varierar således.

rows.title: Circular Economy Geneva
rows.description: This project aims to identify the economic opportunities and challenges for Dutch businesses, businesses in developing countries and developing country's national development strategies towards the circular economy. In the short term, this entails organising and coordinating Dutch input for WTO events in the fall of 2019.The output of the project is also an assessment of CE development in conjunction with WTO rules, together with the WTO secretariat, and contributing to discussions on this topic in the Committee on Trade and Environment of WTO.The project will further develop and establish a network of WTO members committed to creating an international circular economy. The results can be used as input for the World Circular Economy Forum 2021, organised in the Netherlands by the Ministry of Infrastructure and Water Management (MinIenW).

rows.title: Unlocking debate trade labour
rows.description: The main purpose of this project is to unlock, at the multilateral level, the current deadlock in the dialogue on the nexus between trade and labour.The project will culminate in a workshop that will provide a neutral platform for exchange among key stakeholders on the major issues at the interface of trade and labour and an opportunity for concrete and substantive follow up.To facilitate dialogue and ultimately ensure the success of the workshop, background material will be prepared in the form of a background brief and a stakeholder mapping.

rows.reporting: Foreign Affairs, Trade and Development Canada (DFATD)
rows.title: Humber Institute – Stages PSIJ 2015-2016
rows.description: This project is part of the Department of Foreign Affairs, Trade and Development (DFATD) International Youth Internship Program (IYIP), funded by the Government of Canada's Youth Employment Strategy (YES). The IYIP contributes to the YES by providing a large spectrum of Canadian graduates with valuable international development work experience abroad, needed to launch successful careers.   Humber Institute Health and Private Sector Development project provides 20 interns with internships in ten countries: Bangladesh, Haiti, Ethiopia, Malawi, Tanzania, Bolivia, Burkina Faso, Peru, Nicaragua and Colombia. The interns work to address development challenges in relation to local needs by contributing to private sector development and health improvement by strengthening Food Security and Nutrition programming and Water and Sanitation projects.

rows.reporting: Foreign Affairs, Trade and Development Canada (DFATD)
rows.title: Nepal Earthquake - Emergency Response 2015 - Samaritan’s Purse Canada
rows.description: May 2015 - On April 25, a magnitude 7.8 earthquake hit Nepal, with the epicentre 81 km northwest of Kathmandu. The earthquake has affected over 8 million people, displaced 2.8 million, and has left more than 3.5 million Nepalese in need of food assistance. To date, the Government of Nepal has confirmed over 8,600 deaths and 16,800 injured. On May 12, a 7.3 magnitude earthquake struck Nepal, northeast of Kathmandu, and near its border with China. This aftershock caused additional damage and loss of life, including throughout many areas already impacted by the April 25 earthquake.   With DFATD's support, Samaritan’s Purse Canada is helping to meet the shelter, hygiene, and household items needs of up to 30,000 earthquake-affected people. Project activities include: (1) distribution of emergency relief supplies, including tarps, blankets, jerry cans, cooking kits, and hygiene items; and (2) distribution of emergency shelter kits and tools.

rows.reporting: Foreign Affairs, Trade and Development Canada (DFATD)
rows.title: Nepal Earthquake - Emergency Response 2015 - CARE Canada
rows.description: May 2015 - On April 25, a magnitude 7.8 earthquake hit Nepal, with the epicentre 81 km northwest of Kathmandu. The earthquake has affected over 8 million people, displaced 2.8 million, and has left more than 3.5 million Nepalese in need of food assistance. To date, the Government of Nepal has confirmed over 8,600 deaths and 16,800 injured. On May 12, a 7.3 magnitude earthquake struck Nepal, northeast of Kathmandu, and near its border with China. This aftershock caused additional damage and loss of life, including throughout many areas already impacted by the April 25 earthquake.  With DFATD's support, CARE Canada is helping to meet the emergency shelter, water, and hygiene needs of up to 10,000 earthquake affected people. Project activities include: (1) distribution of emergency shelter, household, and hygiene kits; (2) provision of household water containers and water treatment supplies (water purification solutions); and (3) delivery of hygiene promotion training.

rows.reporting: Foreign Affairs, Trade and Development Canada (DFATD)
rows.title: Nepal Earthquake Emergency Response 2015 - Save the Children Canada
rows.description: April 2015 – On 25 April, an earthquake of 7.8 magnitude hit Nepal near the capital city of Kathmandu. The government has reported that 30 out of 75 districts are affected in the Western and Central Regions including districts in Kathmandu Valley. The epicentre is located 81 km northwest of Kathmandu. A number of large aftershocks have occurred since the first earthquake. To date, over 3,000 deaths have been confirmed and over 6,000 people have been injured. These figures are expected to increase as more information becomes available.   With DFATD's support, Save the Children Canada is helping to meet the emergency water, hygiene, and sanitation needs of people affected by the earthquake. Project activities include: (1) providing safe drinking water; (2) distributing hygiene kits; (3) repairing essential water and sanitation facilities; and (4) delivering community educational messages to promote good hygiene practices.

rows.reporting: Foreign Affairs, Trade and Development Canada (DFATD)
rows.title: Nepal Earthquake Emergency Response 2015 - Oxfam Canada
rows.description: April 2015 – On 25 April, an earthquake of 7.8 magnitude hit Nepal near the capital city of Kathmandu. The government has reported that 30 out of 75 districts are affected in the Western and Central Regions, including districts in Kathmandu Valley. The epicentre is located 81 km northwest of Kathmandu. A number of large aftershocks have occurred since the first earthquake. To date, over 3,000 deaths have been confirmed and over 6,000 people have been injured. These figures are expected to increase as more information becomes available.   With DFATD's support, Oxfam Canada is helping to meet emergency water, hygiene, sanitation, and relief supplies needs of people affected by the earthquake. Project activities include: (1) providing safe drinking water; (2) providing sanitation facilities and hygiene promotion at health facilities; and (3) distributing emergency relief supplies, including blankets, water containers and cooking items.

rows.title: IM Swedish Development Partner, 2019-2023 - IM Swedish Development Partner, agreement 2019-2023
rows.description:
Stärkt civilsamhälle genom att IM, som en feministisk och antirasistisk förändringsaktör, katalyserar djupgående samhällsförändringar med fokus på:
Rätt till social inkluderingEnligt IM omfattar socal inkludering rätten till deltagande, möjligheten att fritt bidra till samhället och rätten till icke-diskriminering. Det kan till exempel handla om att människor med funktionsnedsättning inte erhåller det stöd de behöver vad gäller tillgång till utbildning, meningsfull sysselsättning och hälso- och sjukvård. För att uppnå social inkludering kommer IM till exempel i Jordanien att arbeta med partners för att stärka utsatta gruppers kapacitet att utkräva sina rättigheter. IM kommer i Jordanien även att föra dialog med lokala och nationella ledare i syfte att förbättra lagstiftningen och de strukturer som finns för att säkerställa social inkludering. 
Rätt till ekonomisk inkluderingEnligt IM handlar ekonomisk inkludering om att ge marginaliserade grupper möjligheten att ha ägarskap över sitt ekonomiska liv. Målgruppen ska ges möjlighet till en förbättrad ekonomisk situation samtidigt som deras status i samhället stärks.  För att uppnå ekonomisk inkludering kommer IM till exempel i Malawi att fortsätta att stödja "village savings and loan associations" för kvinnor. Vidare kommer IM i Malawi att stödja unga människors initiativ gällande klimatsmarta bevattningssystem som drivs av solenergi. IM Malawi är även medlem i "Movement for Community Led Development - Malawi chapter" som övervakar regeringens åtagande enligt SDG:s 4,5,8 och 9.
Stärkt civilsamhälleIM lyfter fram det civila samhällets betydelse i ett demokratiskt samhälle, som röstbärare och för att stärka medvetenheten ibland målgrupper om deras rättigheter. För att kunna spela denna roll behöver civilsamhället vara starkt och ha tillräcklig kapacitet. En central del i IM:s strategi för att stärka det civila samhället är IM:s program för "organisational and capacity development (OCD)" för partners. 
En nyhet inför 2019-2023 är att IM kommer att uppmuntra partners att avsätta medel i deras budget från IM för organisationsutveckling. Detta till skillnad från tidigare då alla medel dedikerade till kapacitetsutveckling har varit del av IM:s budgetar. IM motiverar dena förändring med att ett erbjudande om stöd som bygger på ett verkligt behov har bättre förutsättningar att vara framgångsrikt än motsatsen. Samtidigt kommer IM:s organisationsbedömningar samt dialogen med partners även fortsättningsvis att att vara en viktig del av det OCD som IM bidrar med till respektive partner.   
IM har även för avsikt att under kommande period stärka möjligheterna för partners att lära av varandra. IM:s nätverksmöten är en del i detta. Vissa partners är även experter inom tematiska områden och kan bidra med utbildning för andra partners. I södra Afrika till exempel har "Just Associates" stark kompetens gällande jämställdhetsfrågor och kan vara en resurs för andra partners i regionen.
IM har valt att prioritera ett antal områden för den kompetensutveckling som IM själva genomför och har budgetansvar för. Det gäller till exempel tematiska tvärfrågor, såsom miljö- och klimat och konflikt, samt anti-korruption, riskhantering och intern styrning och kontroll.    
Säkrat utrymme för det civila samhälletAtt motverka trenden med ett krympande utrymme för det civila samhället att verka är en prioriterad fråga för IM. För att bidra till detta kommer IM till exempel i södra Asien att initiera ett regionalt program om "civic space" och att anordna nätverksmöten för partners tillsammans med landkontoren i Indien och Nepal. I Indien kommer IM även att arbeta för att stärka partners interna kontrollsystem för att möta de krav som ställs av regeringen. Några av IM:s samarbetspartners i Nepal är människorättsorganisationer som driver påverkansinitiativ gällande "civic space."

rows.description:
Stärkt civilsamhälle genom att IM, som en feministisk och antirasistisk förändringsaktör, katalyserar djupgående samhällsförändringar med fokus på:
Rätt till social inkluderingEnligt IM omfattar socal inkludering rätten till deltagande, möjligheten att fritt bidra till samhället och rätten till icke-diskriminering. Det kan till exempel handla om att människor med funktionsnedsättning inte erhåller det stöd de behöver vad gäller tillgång till utbildning, meningsfull sysselsättning och hälso- och sjukvård. För att uppnå social inkludering kommer IM till exempel i Jordanien att arbeta med partners för att stärka utsatta gruppers kapacitet att utkräva sina rättigheter. IM kommer i Jordanien även att föra dialog med lokala och nationella ledare i syfte att förbättra lagstiftningen och de strukturer som finns för att säkerställa social inkludering. 
Rätt till ekonomisk inkluderingEnligt IM handlar ekonomisk inkludering om att ge marginaliserade grupper möjligheten att ha ägarskap över sitt ekonomiska liv. Målgruppen ska ges möjlighet till en förbättrad ekonomisk situation samtidigt som deras status i samhället stärks.  För att uppnå ekonomisk inkludering kommer IM till exempel i Malawi att fortsätta att stödja "village savings and loan associations" för kvinnor. Vidare kommer IM i Malawi att stödja unga människors initiativ gällande klimatsmarta bevattningssystem som drivs av solenergi. IM Malawi är även medlem i "Movement for Community Led Development - Malawi chapter" som övervakar regeringens åtagande enligt SDG:s 4,5,8 och 9.
Stärkt civilsamhälleIM lyfter fram det civila samhällets betydelse i ett demokratiskt samhälle, som röstbärare och för att stärka medvetenheten ibland målgrupper om deras rättigheter. För att kunna spela denna roll behöver civilsamhället vara starkt och ha tillräcklig kapacitet. En central del i IM:s strategi för att stärka det civila samhället är IM:s program för "organisational and capacity development (OCD)" för partners. 
En nyhet inför 2019-2023 är att IM kommer att uppmuntra partners att avsätta medel i deras budget från IM för organisationsutveckling. Detta till skillnad från tidigare då alla medel dedikerade till kapacitetsutveckling har varit del av IM:s budgetar. IM motiverar dena förändring med att ett erbjudande om stöd som bygger på ett verkligt behov har bättre förutsättningar att vara framgångsrikt än motsatsen. Samtidigt kommer IM:s organisationsbedömningar samt dialogen med partners även fortsättningsvis att att vara en viktig del av det OCD som IM bidrar med till respektive partner.   
IM har även för avsikt att under kommande period stärka möjligheterna för partners att lära av varandra. IM:s nätverksmöten är en del i detta. Vissa partners är även experter inom tematiska områden och kan bidra med utbildning för andra partners. I södra Afrika till exempel har "Just Associates" stark kompetens gällande jämställdhetsfrågor och kan vara en resurs för andra partners i regionen.
IM har valt att prioritera ett antal områden för den kompetensutveckling som IM själva genomför och har budgetansvar för. Det gäller till exempel tematiska tvärfrågor, såsom miljö- och klimat och konflikt, samt anti-korruption, riskhantering och intern styrning och kontroll.    
Säkrat utrymme för det civila samhälletAtt motverka trenden med ett krympande utrymme för det civila samhället att verka är en prioriterad fråga för IM. För att bidra till detta kommer IM till exempel i södra Asien att initiera ett regionalt program om "civic space" och att anordna nätverksmöten för partners tillsammans med landkontoren i Indien och Nepal. I Indien kommer IM även att arbeta för att stärka partners interna kontrollsystem för att möta de krav som ställs av regeringen. Några av IM:s samarbetspartners i Nepal är människorättsorganisationer som driver påverkansinitiativ gällande "civic space."

rows.reporting: Foreign Affairs, Trade and Development Canada (DFATD)
rows.title: Nepal Earthquake - Emergency Response 2015 - CECI
rows.description: May 2015 - On April 25, a magnitude 7.8 earthquake hit Nepal, with the epicentre 81 km northwest of Kathmandu. The earthquake has affected over 8 million people, displaced 2.8 million, and has left more than 3.5 million Nepalese in need of food assistance. To date, the Government of Nepal has confirmed over 8,600 deaths and 16,800 injured. On May 12, a 7.3 magnitude earthquake struck Nepal, northeast of Kathmandu, and near its border with China. This aftershock caused additional damage and loss of life, including throughout many areas already impacted by the April 25 earthquake.   With DFATD's support, the Centre for International Studies and Cooperation (CECI) is helping to meet the water, sanitation, hygiene and shelter needs, and support the livelihoods of up to 25,000 people affected by the earthquake. Project activities include: (1) distributing emergency shelter kits; (2) providing emergency relief supplies and water, sanitation, and hygiene (WASH) items; (3) providing psychosocial support and referral services; and (4) training to households on livestock and agriculture management.

rows.reporting: Foreign Affairs, Trade and Development Canada (DFATD)
rows.title: Nepal Earthquake Emergency Response 2015 - Global Medic
rows.description: April 2015 – On 25 April, an earthquake of 7.8 magnitude hit Nepal near the capital city of Kathmandu. The government has reported that 30 out of 75 districts are affected in the Western and Central Regions, including districts in Kathmandu Valley. The epicentre is located 81 km northwest of Kathmandu. A number of large aftershocks have occurred since the first earthquake. To date, over 3,000 deaths have been confirmed and over 6,000 people have been injured. These figures are expected to increase as more information becomes available.   With DFATD's support, Global Medic is helping to meet the emergency shelter, water, and protection needs of up to 5,000 families affected by the earthquake. Project activities include: (1) providing 30 tents to clinics, hospitals, schools and government buildings to replace damaged infrastructures; (2) providing psychosocial support and referral services for children; (3) distributing water purification units and hygiene kits; and (4) deploying Unmanned Aerial Vehicles (UAV) to map and assess damaged areas in need of humanitarian assistance.

rows.reporting: Foreign Affairs, Trade and Development Canada (DFATD)
rows.title: Nepal Earthquake Emergency Response 2015 - World Vision Canada
rows.description: April 2015 – Le 25 avril, un séisme de magnitude 7,8 a frappé le Népal près de Katmandou, la capitale. Le gouvernement a indiqué que 30 districts sur 75 sont touchés dans les régions de l’Ouest et du Centre, y compris les districts de la vallée de Katmandou. L’épicentre est situé à 81 km au nord-ouest de Katmandou. Un certain nombre de répliques importantes se sont produites depuis le premier séisme. À ce jour, plus de 3 000 décès ont été confirmés et plus de 6 000 personnes ont été blessées. On s’attend à ce que ces chiffres augmentent à mesure que de l’information additionnelle sera disponible.   Avec le soutien du MAECD, Vision mondiale Canada aide à répondre aux besoins des personnes touchées par le séisme en matière d’abris, de fournitures de secours d’urgence et de protection. Parmi les activités du projet : 1) la fourniture d’abris d’urgence, y compris des tentes, des nattes et des couvertures aux familles touchées; 2) la distribution d’articles de secours d’urgence aux familles, y compris des vêtements chauds, des lanternes tempête et du savon; 3) la mise en place de six espaces adaptés aux enfants; 4) la fourniture de services de soutien psychosocial et d’aiguillage pour les enfants.

rows.reporting: Foreign Affairs, Trade and Development Canada (DFATD)
rows.title: Nepal Earthquake – Emergency Response 2015 – Emergency Field Hospital – Canadian Red Cross
rows.description: May 2015 – On 25 April, an earthquake of 7.8 magnitude hit Nepal near the capital city of Kathmandu. The government has reported that 30 out of 75 districts are affected in the Western and Central Regions, including districts in Kathmandu Valley. The epicentre is located 81 km northwest of Kathmandu. A number of large aftershocks have occurred since the first earthquake. To date, over 3,000 deaths have been confirmed and over 6,000 people have been injured. These figures are expected to increase as more information becomes available.   With DFATD’s support, the Canadian Red Cross (CRC) is helping to meet the life-saving health needs of up to 50,000 people affected by the earthquake. Relief activities include: (1) deploying a basic health care unit with surgical and obstetric care capacity; and (2) providing mobile health care, including vaccinations, rehydration, and mother and child care for up to 200 people per day.

rows.reporting: Foreign Affairs, Trade and Development Canada (DFATD)
rows.title: Nepal Earthquake Emergency Response 2015 - IFRC
rows.description: April 2015 – On 25 April, an earthquake of 7.8 magnitude hit Nepal near the capital city of Kathmandu. The government has reported that 30 out of 75 districts are affected in the Western and Central Regions, including districts in Kathmandu Valley. The epicentre is located 81 km northwest of Kathmandu. A number of large aftershocks have occurred since the first earthquake. To date, over 3,000 deaths have been confirmed and over 6,000 people have been injured. These figures are expected to increase as more information becomes available.   With the support of DFATD and other donors, the International Federation of Red Cross and Red Crescent Societies (IFRC) is working alongside the Nepal Red Cross Society (NRC) to help meet the life-saving needs of up to up to 75,000 people affected by the earthquake. Relief activities include: (1) setting up emergency health facilities and providing basic medical treatment; (2) providing safe drinking water and rehabilitating water points; and (3) providing emergency relief supplies, including shelter kits, blankets, jerry cans, hygiene kits and kitchen sets.

rows.reporting: Foreign Affairs, Trade and Development Canada (DFATD)
rows.title: Nepal Earthquake 2015 - Deployment of Emergency Relief Items – Canadian Red Cross
rows.description: May 2015 - On 25 April, a magnitude 7.8 earthquake hit Nepal, with the epicentre 81 km northwest of Kathmandu. The earthquake has affected over 8 million people, displaced 2.8 million, and has left more than 3.5 million Nepalese in need of food assistance. On 12 May, a 7.3 magnitude earthquake struck Nepal, northeast of Kathmandu, and near its border with China. This aftershock caused additional damage and loss of life, including throughout many areas already impacted by the April 25 earthquake.   In response to the crisis, DFATD is deploying emergency relief items from its stockpile, managed by the Canadian Red Cross, to help meet the immediate needs of up to 25,000 affected people. The relief items are being provided to those who are most in need, in coordination with the Government of Nepal and the wider humanitarian community. The relief items include: (1) tents and tarpaulins to help provide temporary shelter; and (2) basic household items such as blankets, kitchen sets, and hygiene kits (which include items such as soap and toothbrushes), as well as jerry cans and buckets to safely transport water.

rows.reporting: Foreign Affairs, Trade and Development Canada (DFATD)
rows.title: Nepal Earthquake Emergency Response 2015 – OCHA
rows.description: May 2015 – On 25 April, an earthquake of 7.8 magnitude hit Nepal near the capital city of Kathmandu. The government has reported that 30 out of 75 districts are affected in the Western and Central Regions, including districts in Kathmandu Valley. The epicentre is located 81 km northwest of Kathmandu. A number of large aftershocks have occurred since the first earthquake. To date, over 5,000 deaths have been confirmed and over 11,000 people have been injured. These figures are expected to increase as more information becomes available.  With DFATD's support and that of other donors, the United Nations Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs (OCHA) is providing essential coordination support for the humanitarian response in Nepal. Project activities include: (1) providing assessments, analysis, information management and coordination assistance to support the Government of Nepal’s response to the disaster; (2) providing coordination support to local, national and international humanitarian organizations; (3) providing information to affected populations on the response efforts to improve their ability to access help; and (4) collecting feedback from affected communities to ensure their experience informs the wider humanitarian response.

rows.reporting: Foreign Affairs, Trade and Development Canada (DFATD)
rows.title: Nepal Earthquake - Emergency Response 2015 - UNICEF
rows.description: May 2015 – On 25 April, an earthquake of 7.8 magnitude hit Nepal near the capital city of Kathmandu. The government has reported that 30 out of 75 districts are affected in the Western and Central Regions, including districts in Kathmandu Valley. The epicentre is located 81 km northwest of Kathmandu. A number of large aftershocks have occurred since the first earthquake. To date, over 5,000 deaths have been confirmed and over 11,000 people have been injured. These figures are expected to increase as more information becomes available.  With DFATD's support and that of other donors, the United Nations Children’s Fund (UNICEF) is helping to meet the emergency water, sanitation, hygiene, nutrition, and protection needs of people affected by the earthquake. Project activities include: (1) providing safe drinking water to people in informal camps; (2) distributing hygiene kits, including soap, sanitary towels and detergent; (3) delivering tents and tarpaulins to serve as emergency shelters for families; (4) establishing safe spaces for children and women; (5) providing psychological support to children in informal camps; (6) reunifying separated children with their families; (7) providing emergency school kits for children in temporary learning centres; (8) ensuring access to programmes to treat and prevent acute malnutrition in vulnerable population groups; and (9) providing protection services and counselling to vulnerable and at-risk populations.

rows.reporting: Foreign Affairs, Trade and Development Canada (DFATD)
rows.title: Nepal Earthquake - Emergency Food Assistance - World Food Programme 2015
rows.description: May 2015 – On 25 April, an earthquake of 7.8 magnitude hit Nepal near the capital city of Kathmandu. The government has reported that 30 out of 75 districts are affected in the Western and Central Regions, including districts in Kathmandu Valley. The epicentre is located 81 km northwest of Kathmandu. A number of large aftershocks have occurred since the first earthquake. With the support of DFATD and other donors, the World Food Programme (WFP) is helping to meet the emergency food needs of up to 1.4 million earthquake-affected people in Nepal. Project activities include: (1) providing emergency food assistance to earthquake-affected households; (2) providing targeted nutrition assistance for children under-five and pregnant and lactating women; and (3) coordinating food security and livelihoods activities and assessments.

rows.reporting: Foreign Affairs, Trade and Development Canada (DFATD)
rows.title: Nepal Earthquake - Emergency Logistics Support - World Food Programme 2015

Activities include: (1) providing logistics and storage services, strategic airlifts and in-country cargo transportation for the humanitarian response; and (2) providing emergency telecommunications and information management services to support the humanitarian response.
